meta.model: claude-sonnet-5 (manual, no API key — see CLAUDE.md v4 workaround)

digests.narrative:
His highest-conviction, highest-concentration theme by a wide margin. Core thesis: NVIDIA's move to 1.6T optics and co-packaged optics (CPO) forces an architectural shift from EML to continuous-wave (CW) lasers, and Sivers ($SIVE) is the under-recognized CW/DFB laser bottleneck feeding the entire hyperscaler CPO ecosystem — GlobalFoundries' reference design, Ayar/NVLink scale-up, Jabil's 1.6T pluggables, POET's scale-out, O-Net's ELS. He holds a million-plus share position and has tracked a string of validating catalysts over June-July: JP Morgan's stake rising 0.4%->5%+, an emergency $70M raise for mass-production capacity, a Win Semi allocation confirmation, a $8.2M space/defense order, Fidelity/BlackRock institutional ownership, EU CHIPS Act 2.0 backing, and a confirmed intent to complete a Nasdaq listing within a few quarters.

Around Sivers he tracks XFAB (a pre-commercial European silicon-photonics foundry he calls 'early TSEM,' validated by NVDA/Nokia evaluations and EU policy support at a market cap he repeatedly calls a joke), AAOI (Made-in-America CW capacity projecting ~$1.4B/quarter run-rate by H1 2027, 800%+ Y/Y growth), IQE (InP epiwafer chokepoint supplier, de-risked by a multi-year TSEM deal), LITE and COHR (top-three laser suppliers 'completely sold out' into 2028-2029), JBL, POET, and MTSI (invested directly in IQE to secure epiwafer capacity). He treats a SemiAnalysis 'CPO delay' report plus a rival ETF launch as manufactured FUD the sector refuted, and reads the recent selloff as noise against a TAM scaling from near-zero to ~$91B by 2026-2028.
keyPoints: Sivers ($SIVE) is the recurring CW-laser chokepoint across GFS, Ayar/NVLink, Jabil 1.6T pluggables, POET scale-out, and O-Net — JPM stake rose 0.4%->5%+, plus a $70M raise, a Win Semi allocation win, and Nasdaq-listing intent, all in the past month.

XFAB is his #2 photonics conviction: a pre-commercial European silicon-photonics foundry likened to 'early TSEM,' backed by EU CHIPS Act 2.0 and NVDA/Nokia evaluations at a market cap he calls too cheap ahead of 2027-2028 volume ramps.

AAOI: Made-in-America CW capacity projected near $1.4B/quarter run-rate by H1 2027 (800%+ Y/Y); IQE is the InP epiwafer chokepoint feeding the same laser ecosystem, de-risked by a multi-year TSEM supply deal.
Top-three laser suppliers (LITE, COHR, plus Sivers) control ~68% of the market and are sold out into 2028-2029 per POET's AGM and Lumentum's own CEO.
A SemiAnalysis report framing CPO as delayed (paired with a rival ETF launch after a 40-60% optical drawdown) is read as manufactured FUD the sector refuted.
Timing map: 1.6T pluggables now->H1 2027, CPO scale-out late H2 2026, NPO H2 2026/2027, CPO scale-up H2 2027 — TAM scaling from near-zero to ~$91B over that window.

digests.narrative:
Anchored on AXTI as a multi-month InP-substrate bottleneck thesis: China's export-control easing on InP substrates is read as a direct capacity tailwind (also benefiting AAOI/IQE/LITE/SIVE downstream), while AXTI's own re-rating ($13->$140s) came with a float expansion and dilution he flags as a real risk, alongside a new 3-year wafer supply deal with Coherent ($22.29M prepayment for committed capacity). He stresses patience — 'don't judge a multi-month theme on short timeframes.'

SanDisk (SNDK) and Micron (MU) are the other core memory names — SNDK shorts are described as 'wiped out' with the stock surging toward $2,000, and his own MU gross-margin model (pricing in DRAM/NAND hikes ahead of time) was validated by results, with the AI capex cycle expected to run through 2028. He also flags RPI (~247% gains since his thesis, AI-driven memory demand pushing profit ahead of expectations) and WUS TW (Winbond) as a NAV-arbitrage-plus-AI-growth trade with activist involvement as a catalyst. A tungsten (WF6) export-control bottleneck out of China (hitting SK Hynix, Samsung, and TSM) is a recurring research thread pointing to upstream supply risk across the whole memory chain.
keyPoints: AXTI: multi-month InP-substrate bottleneck thesis; China's export easing is a direct capacity tailwind, but the $13->$140s re-rating came with float expansion/dilution worth watching. New 3-year Coherent wafer deal ($22.29M prepayment for committed capacity).
SanDisk (SNDK) shorts are 'wiped out' with the stock surging toward $2,000 on AI-driven memory demand.
Micron (MU): his own gross-margin model (pricing in DRAM/NAND hikes ahead of time) was validated by results; AI capex cycle expected to run through 2028.
RPI: ~247% gains since his thesis, with AI-driven memory demand pushing profit ahead of expectations.
Winbond (WUS TW): NAV-arbitrage-plus-AI-growth trade, with activist involvement and an H-share listing flagged as catalysts.
Recurring bottleneck research: a China tungsten (WF6) export control disrupting SK Hynix/Samsung/TSM supply chains.

digests.narrative:
GlobalFoundries (GFS) is the connective tissue here — its silicon-photonics reference design embeds Sivers lasers as the default light source across the NVDA CPO ecosystem, and he's constructive on it as a 'quality holding' even as its size limits further multibagger potential. TSM is read as aggressively building a silicon-photonics/CPO empire through subsidiaries Xintec (testing) and VisEra (optical foundry), with the chairman confirming CoPoS pilot lines running now for 2-3 year volume — a longer runway than the market's 'heavily delayed' narrative suggests; autonomous vehicles/robots are flagged as a new TSM growth area.

Equipment names (ASML, Applied Materials, Lam Research, KLA, Veeco) recur as the unglamorous but unavoidable layer under every advanced node and photonics wafer — Veeco's MBE tools specifically grow the InP/GaAs laser wafers feeding the CPO chokepoint. NAV-discount Asian equipment names (ACMR, Camtek) are framed as standalone-growth ideas via China-listed subsidiaries and H-share listings. He also flags OpenAI's reported talks to give the US government a stake as echoing the Intel playbook — a potential soft government backstop for hyperscaler capex — and a tungsten (WF6) export-control bottleneck touching Japan/Korea fab-adjacent supply chains.
keyPoints: GlobalFoundries (GFS): its silicon-photonics reference design embeds Sivers lasers as the default across the NVDA CPO ecosystem; a quality holding, though now large enough to cap multibagger upside.

TSM is aggressively building a silicon-photonics/CPO empire via subsidiaries Xintec (testing) and VisEra (optical foundry); CoPoS pilot lines are running now for 2-3 year volume, with autonomous vehicles/robots flagged as a new growth area.

Equipment names (ASML, Applied Materials, Lam Research, KLA) are the unavoidable layer under every advanced node; Veeco's MBE tools specifically grow the InP/GaAs laser wafers feeding the photonics chokepoint.
NAV-discount Asian equipment plays (ACMR, Camtek) are framed as standalone-growth ideas via China-listed subsidiaries and H-share listings.
OpenAI's reported talks to give the US government a stake echo the Intel playbook — a potential soft government backstop for hyperscaler capex.
A tungsten (WF6) export-control bottleneck out of China is a recurring watch item for Japan/Korea fab-adjacent supply chains.

digests.narrative:
High conviction that the 'Meta is cutting capex' narrative is disinformation. His read: Google restricted Meta's internal compute allocation back in March because Meta's own usage was too high, forcing Meta to immediately sign $48B+ contracts with neoclouds like CoreWeave and Nebius — a demand-constraint story, not a demand-weakening one. Nebius (NBIS) is his highest-conviction pure-play neocloud, 'execution certainty delivering asymmetric upside,' repeatedly validated by all-time highs and a Nasdaq-100 addition; he explicitly frames switching from Iris Energy to Nebius last year as the correct call.

The bitcoin-miner-to-neocloud pivot (Iris Energy, Cipher Mining, TeraWulf) is a recurring secondary thread — Anthropic's data-center lease news is cited as a tailwind for WULF/CIFR colo capacity, though he holds a negative view on IREN specifically over its GPU-pivot-from-colo narrative and ongoing $6B ATM dilution. He also cites Wells Fargo's read that Meta's own excess-compute resale plans validate rather than threaten the neocloud opportunity.
keyPoints: Google restricting Meta's internal compute allocation in March forced Meta's $48B+ neocloud contracts (CRWV, NBIS) — a demand-constraint story, not a 'cutting capex' story.

Nebius (NBIS) is his highest-conviction pure-play neocloud — 'execution certainty delivering asymmetric upside' — validated by all-time highs and a Nasdaq-100 addition; switching from Iris Energy to Nebius last year is framed as the correct call.

Bitcoin-miner-to-neocloud pivot (Cipher Mining, TeraWulf, Iris Energy): Anthropic's data-center lease news is a tailwind for colo capacity, but he holds a negative view on IREN specifically over its GPU-pivot narrative and ongoing $6B ATM dilution.
Wells Fargo: Meta's excess-compute resale plans validate the AI-infra opportunity for neoclouds rather than threatening it.

digests.narrative:
Lighter-touch theme, mostly picks-and-shovels research. AEHR's ~100% rally is viewed as largely priced in, with limited near-term re-rating room absent a new catalyst. LPKF (LPK.DE) is a recurring bullish idea on its glass-core substrate monopoly — >80% of major global players selecting its equipment for validation, a 70% market-share target, and NASDAQ-listing discussions — with a stated $3-5B fair value at full volume ramp. Soitec (SOI.PA) is repeatedly cited as a European chokepoint SOI-wafer supplier benefiting from EU CHIPS Act 2.0's new photonics-policy priority, alongside LPKF and XFAB.

ASE Technology (ASX) and Teradyne (TER) round out the packaging/test layer, mentioned inside broader '$10-100B AI-exposed names' baskets as offering compelling ROI versus larger names. Ongoing trade-record research (Starlink and other direct-supplier mapping) continues to surface obscure materials names, and the China->Japan->Korea tungsten (WF6) export-control thread remains a recurring materials-bottleneck watch item.
keyPoints: LPKF (LPK.DE): glass-core substrate monopoly, >80% major-player validation, 70% market-share target, NASDAQ-listing discussions — stated $3-5B fair value at full volume ramp.
Soitec (SOI.PA): European SOI-wafer chokepoint supplier, a repeated beneficiary of EU CHIPS Act 2.0's new photonics-policy priority.
AEHR's ~100% rally is seen as mostly priced in — limited near-term re-rating room without a new catalyst.
ASE Technology (ASX) and Teradyne (TER) round out the packaging/test layer, cited inside broader AI-exposed-name baskets for compelling ROI.
The China->Japan->Korea tungsten (WF6) export-control thread remains a recurring materials-bottleneck watch item.

digests.narrative:
Centers on a half-serious 'conspiracy theory' that Nvidia is discretely backing ASIC challengers — Marvell (MRVL), MediaTek, AlChip, GUC (NVLink) — to chip away at Broadcom's (AVGO) custom-silicon share, the same way neocloud offerings created a moat against CSPs' custom ASICs last year. Broadcom itself is described as structurally bullish on ~2028 networking-demand visibility despite this multi-sourcing pressure. Jensen Huang's 'the next $1T company' comment continues to anchor sentiment through MRVL drawdowns; he holds shares and also flags MRVL's CPO and Maia-ASIC exposure as reaffirming his favorable view.

Astera Labs (ALAB) — PCIe/CXL connectivity linking GPUs, CPUs and memory — was added with pleasure on its Nasdaq-100 inclusion, though he later noted reduced conviction. Nokia (NOK) recurs as a validating customer alongside NVDA evaluating XFAB's silicon-photonics foundry for CPO applications.
keyPoints: Half-serious thesis: Nvidia is discretely backing ASIC challengers (Marvell, MediaTek, AlChip, GUC) to chip away at Broadcom's custom-silicon share.
Broadcom (AVGO) is structurally bullish on ~2028 networking-demand visibility despite multi-sourcing pressure from the ASIC-challenger cohort.
Jensen Huang's 'the next $1T company' comment continues to anchor sentiment through MRVL drawdowns; also holds shares for its CPO and Maia-ASIC exposure.
Astera Labs (ALAB): PCIe/CXL connectivity between GPUs/CPUs/memory, added with pleasure on its Nasdaq-100 inclusion, though conviction has since eased.
Nokia (NOK) recurs as a validating customer (alongside NVDA) evaluating XFAB's silicon-photonics foundry for CPO applications.

digests.narrative: A new, thin entry in this cycle: Corning (GLW) surfaced only inside a broader Bloomberg-driven semis-selloff basket (down ~11.4% alongside AAOI/MRVL/LITE/SNDK/TER on a report UBS called 'not new news'). No dedicated glass-substrate thesis has been posted on it yet — it's tracked here as a natural candidate for the glass-core advanced-packaging wave (alongside SKC Absolics/TSMC CoPoS) given Corning's position as the leading specialty-glass and optical-fiber maker, but that connection hasn't been stated directly by the analyst.
keyPoints: Corning (GLW) has only appeared inside a broader semis-selloff basket so far — no dedicated glass-substrate thesis posted yet.
Tracked as a natural fit for the glass-core advanced-packaging wave given Corning's specialty-glass/fiber leadership, though the analyst hasn't drawn that link explicitly.

digests.narrative:
High conviction, and his largest position by his own account: Agility Robotics (CCXI, renaming to AGLT on its ~September IPO) is 'literally the #1 humanoid player in the US in terms of commercialization today,' ranked ahead of Tesla Optimus and Figure ($39B last round). BlueCrest Capital / Michael Platt disclosed a 5.6% stake in CCXI — institutional validation he'd been expecting. His bull case: ~75% US-sourced parts, a <$30K bill-of-materials target (vs. a ~$145K sticker price people over-focus on), and 10,000-unit RoboFab capacity across Salem, Pittsburgh and Fremont, backed by Amazon, SoftBank, Foxconn and Nvidia — with a stated pre-IPO valuation around $4.9-5.5B, versus Figure's $39B and Unitree's $6.5B IPO valuation.

He frames the field via an LLM-era analogy — Agility is 'Anthropic for robotics,' Optimus is xAI, Figure is ChatGPT/Microsoft — and cites China's 54%+ share of global robotics deployments as the reason a 'national robotics strategy' narrative is building. He prefers CCXI's valuation discipline over richer SPAC-adjacent peers (BOT, trading well above NAV; BRUN and WLAC, other humanoid-IPO vehicles). Sivers lasers reportedly power Boston Dynamics' Atlas via LG Innotek and the Nvidia Hyperion physical-AI ecosystem, and Aeva/Ouster LiDAR sensing rounds out the autonomy-sensing layer.
keyPoints: Agility Robotics (CCXI -> AGLT on IPO) is his largest position — 'the #1 US humanoid player in commercialization today,' ranked ahead of Tesla Optimus and Figure.
BlueCrest Capital / Michael Platt disclosed a 5.6% stake in CCXI, an institutional validation signal.
Bull case: ~75% US-sourced parts, targeting a <$30K BOM (vs. ~$145K sticker), 10,000-unit RoboFab capacity across three US sites, at a stated pre-IPO valuation of ~$4.9-5.5B vs. Figure's $39B.
Frames the field via an LLM-era analogy: Agility is 'Anthropic for robotics,' Optimus is xAI, Figure is ChatGPT/Microsoft.
China's 54%+ share of global robotics deployments is driving a 'national robotics strategy' narrative in the US; prefers CCXI's valuation discipline over richer SPAC peers like BOT.
Sivers lasers reportedly power Boston Dynamics' Atlas via LG Innotek; Aeva and Ouster LiDAR round out the autonomy-sensing layer.

digests.narrative:
High conviction that the CW-laser chokepoint is an Nvidia-and-AMD-shared problem: both are hunting for long-term laser agreements as the top-three suppliers run sold out into 2028-2029. AAOI's 800%+ Y/Y growth case is explicitly tied to AMD's CW LTA activity and ~$471M/month demand scaling into 2028. He treats a SemiAnalysis report framing CPO as delayed — paired with a rival optics ETF launch right after a 40-60% optical drawdown — as manufactured FUD that Nvidia and analyst desks refuted, and frames the AI compute race in explicitly geopolitical terms (a 'modern cold war') as the reason a hyperscaler capex pullback is hard to imagine.

Beyond NVDA/AMD, he holds a starter position in Cerebras (CBRS) — OpenAI's 5.6 Sol model reportedly runs on Cerebras hardware at up to 750 tokens/sec, picked up below the $185 IPO price, though he flags it as a bit overvalued versus profitable peers. Arm (ARM) is noted for substantial gains already with room to run, and Qualcomm (QCOM) is grouped with the AVGO/MRVL ASIC-challenger cohort as it pushes into AI datacenter inference silicon.
keyPoints: AMD and Nvidia are both hunting for CW-laser long-term agreements as the top-three laser suppliers run sold out into 2028-2029.
AAOI's 800%+ Y/Y growth case is tied directly to AMD's CW LTA activity and ~$471M/month demand scaling into 2028.
A SemiAnalysis report framing CPO as delayed (paired with a rival ETF launch after a 40-60% optical drawdown) is read as manufactured FUD that Nvidia and analyst desks refuted.

Cerebras (CBRS): starter position picked up below the $185 IPO price after OpenAI's 5.6 Sol model reportedly ran on Cerebras hardware at up to 750 tokens/sec; flagged as a bit overvalued versus profitable peers.
Arm (ARM) noted for substantial gains already with room to run; Qualcomm (QCOM) grouped with the AVGO/MRVL ASIC-challenger cohort pushing into AI datacenter inference.
Frames the AI compute buildout as a 'modern cold war,' making a hyperscaler capex pullback hard to imagine.

digests.narrative:
High conviction that hyperscaler capex holds, built on the same read as his neoclouds thesis: Google restricted Meta's internal compute allocation in March, forcing Meta's $48B+ neocloud contracts — a compute-constraint story, not a 'Meta cutting capex' story the market misread. Amazon is explicitly his favorite hyperscaler: ~1.57M headcount as an LLM-automation opportunity feeding into physical AI (self-driving delivery, warehouse robotics) on top of AWS's own Trainium compute buildout.

He also flags Meta's Vistara architecture (older DDR4 memory with CXL expansion) as active cost engineering under compute constraints, is comparatively lukewarm ('iffy') on Oracle's (ORCL) capex-driven cloud upside versus Amazon, and leans on geopolitical framing — China's claimed 'AI cyber nuclear weapon' capability — to argue a broad hyperscaler capex pullback is very unlikely while the AI race is read as a modern cold war.
keyPoints: Google restricting Meta's internal compute allocation in March forced Meta's $48B+ neocloud contracts — a compute-constraint story, not a 'cutting capex' story.
Amazon is his favorite hyperscaler: ~1.57M headcount as an LLM-automation opportunity, feeding into physical AI (delivery, warehouse robotics) plus AWS Trainium buildout.
Meta's Vistara architecture (older DDR4 + CXL memory expansion) is read as active cost engineering under compute constraints.
Comparatively lukewarm ('iffy') on Oracle's (ORCL) capex-driven cloud upside versus Amazon.
Geopolitical AI-race framing (China's capability claims) is used to argue a hyperscaler capex pullback is unlikely.
